topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee in my mug suddenly felt scalding, a physical manifestation of the sudden chill that had swept through my chest. I stared at Liam, his easy smile fixed on something just beyond my shoulder, and the air seemed to thin around us. "Wait," I managed, the word coming out as a reedy whisper. I took a sharp, shallow breath, trying to anchor myself to the familiar landscape of our shared history, but the ground beneath my feet felt suddenly unsteady. Liam turned, his expression shifting from pleasant engagement to mild confusion. "What is it, Maya?" he asked, his voice warm, entirely unburdened by the weight of shared memory. I felt a strange, hollow sensation bloom in my stomach, like a sudden drop in temperature. Every nerve ending in my arms felt hyper-aware, buzzing with an anxious energy I couldn't seem to settle. I gripped the ceramic mug so tightly my knuckles bleached white.

[story 2]

It happened over lukewarm pizza and the easy rhythm of late-night laughter. I watched Liam recount a story about a summer trip, a memory I’d meticulously cataloged in my mind for years, complete with inside jokes and shared silences. When he paused, searching for a specific detail, his eyes swept over me, blank and polite. He frowned slightly, a small furrow appearing between his brows, as if trying to access a file that simply wasn't there. The noise of the apartment—the hum of the refrigerator, the distant traffic—all seemed to amplify into a deafening roar in my ears. I pushed my chair back abruptly, the scrape of the legs against the wooden floor echoing loudly in the silence that followed his admission. My throat tightened, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I wanted to bolt, to escape the suffocating reality of his indifference, but my feet seemed rooted to the spot, paralyzed by the sheer unexpectedness of the void he had revealed.

[story 3]

I tried to keep my tone level, a practiced calm veneer stretched thin over a churning core of disbelief. "Liam, are you sure you don't remember that camping trip last year?" I repeated the question, my voice pitched a little higher than intended. He looked genuinely perplexed, tilting his head as if I were speaking a foreign language. There was no flicker of recognition in his eyes, just a gentle, distant uncertainty. The casual nature of his dismissal hit me like a physical blow, knocking the air out of my lungs. A strange, cold pressure built behind my eyes, and I could feel a tremor running through my hands, which I instinctively brought up to clutch against my jeans. I forced a smile, a brittle, unnatural thing that felt alien on my face, desperate to mask the sudden, sharp vulnerability exposing itself in my posture.
